Title: Significance of NatB-mediated N-terminal acetylation of auxin biosynthetic enzymes in maintaining auxin homeostasis in Arabidopsis thaliana

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required to maintain normal endogenous IAA levels; loss of function causes auxin-deficient phenotypes and reduced IAA/metabolites
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CKRC3/TCU2 encodes the auxiliary subunit Naa25 of the Arabidopsis NatB complex. 2. Loss of CKRC3 function causes auxin-deficient developmental phenotypes, weaker DR5 reporter activity, and reduced endogenous free IAA and related metabolites. 3. CKRC3 interacts with NBC to form NatB, which mediates N-terminal acetylation of YUC proteins, especially YUC8, to stabilize them and thereby maintain auxin homeostasis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Map-based cloning; allelic analysis; molecular complementation; mutant phenotyping; DR5::GUS/DR5::GFP reporter analysis; IAA/metabolite measurements; Y2H; co-immunoprecipitation; subcellular colocalization
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: NatB auxiliary subunit Naa25; tetratricopeptide repeat (TPR)-containing protein; NatB domain
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Widely expressed in tissues throughout plant development
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Widely expressed in tissues throughout plant development
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 0.1 μM trans-zeatin treatment used to reveal root curling phenotype; exogenous IAA rescue
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS/MS for N-terminal acetylation analysis; no public accession reported
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating NatB auxiliary function could modulate auxin homeostasis through post-translational stabilization of YUC enzymes, but likely with broad pleiotropic effects requiring caution
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of function causes reduced growth, leaf reticulation, early flowering, aborted ovules, short siliques, impaired apical hooks, root defects
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts post-translationally on auxin biosynthetic enzymes rather than as a biosynthetic enzyme itself; also named TCU2

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required to maintain normal endogenous IAA levels via NatB activity; nbc mutants phenocopy auxin deficiency and impair YUC8 stability
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NBC encodes the catalytic subunit Naa20 of Arabidopsis NatB. 2. NBC interacts with CKRC3/TCU2 to form a cytoplasmic NatB complex required for normal auxin homeostasis. 3. Purified NBC acetylates YUC8 N-terminal peptide in vitro, supporting NatB-mediated stabilization of YUC proteins and maintenance of IAA biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping; Y2H; co-immunoprecipitation; subcellular localization; in vitro NAT assay with synthetic peptides; LC-MS/MS
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: NatB catalytic subunit Naa20; N-terminal acetyltransferase
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Widely expressed in tissues throughout plant development (NatB complex context)
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Widely expressed in tissues throughout plant development (NatB complex context)
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 0.1 μM trans-zeatin treatment used to reveal root curling phenotype; exogenous IAA rescue
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS/MS for peptide acetylation analysis; no public accession reported
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A potential lever to tune auxin output by controlling N-terminal acetylation and stability of multiple YUC enzymes, but broad developmental effects are likely
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of function causes auxin-deficient pleiotropic growth defects
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Catalytic component of NatB but not classified here as pathway gene because it modifies biosynthetic enzymes rather than catalyzing an auxin-pathway chemical conversion

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Auxin biosynthesis via the IPyA pathway
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Rate-limiting step; downstream of TAA/TAA1 and upstream of IAA formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts IPyA to IAA as a YUCCA flavin monooxygenase step in the IPyA auxin pathway
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Established as rate-limiting in the paper
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes IAA biosynthesis; YUC8 overexpression causes high-auxin phenotypes in WT, whereas loss of NatB-dependent N-terminal acetylation destabilizes YUC8 and impairs its auxin-biosynthetic function
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Among YUC family genes, yuc8 loss-of-function displayed a ckrc-like auxin-deficient phenotype, making YUC8 the focal auxin biosynthetic enzyme studied. 2. YUC8 is an in vitro and in vivo NatB substrate, and its N-terminal acetylation depends on CKRC3/TCU2-NBC. 3. NatB-mediated N-terminal acetylation stabilizes YUC8 protein without notably changing transcript abundance. 4. Mutation of the second residue E2A reduces YUC8 abundance and abolishes strong auxin-related phenotypes despite retained enzymatic activity in vitro.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping; overexpression in WT and ckrc3 nbc-1; qRT-PCR; western blot; transient protoplast expression; in vitro enzyme assay; in vitro NAT assay; in vivo LC-MS/MS of N-terminal acetylation; rescue assay in yuc8
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: YUCCA flavin monooxygenase-like protein; FMO family
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: 0.1 μM trans-zeatin used in phenotypic assays; standard long-day growth conditions
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S promoter (constitutive) for overexpression and fusion constructs
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS/MS for in vitro and in vivo N-terminal acetylation detection; HPLC quantification of IAA in enzyme assay; no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: YUC8 is a strong engineering target to elevate auxin, but stable output depends on preserving NatB-compatible N-terminus; engineering should avoid N-terminal changes that impair acetylation and stability
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression causes high-auxin phenotypes such as long hypocotyl and epinastic cotyledons; altered auxin levels likely affect multiple developmental traits
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Eight of eleven Arabidopsis YUC proteins carry NatB substrate signatures, suggesting wider NatB control beyond YUC8
